Executive Summary

  • Immediate conflict: US and Israeli strikes across Iran with reciprocal Iranian missile/drone launches affecting Gulf and neighbouring states.
  • Current priority theatres: Parand (near Tehran), Doha, Qatar, Nakhchivan, Azerbaijan.

Known Kinetic Events

  • US/Israeli air strikes: Multiple strikes reported inside Iran; media show widespread destruction.
  • Missile barrage on Qatar: Loud explosions over Doha consistent with an Iranian missile salvo.
  • Drone attack in Azerbaijan exclave: Suspected Iranian drone strike at Nakhchivan airport; Tehran denies responsibility.
  • Educational infrastructure struck: Two schools hit near Parand (Tehran area); recent single attack reported 165 fatalities.

Critical Infrastructure & Civilian Impact

  • Communications: Internet restrictions reported inside Iran; potential information blackouts.
  • Water / Electricity: Damage to facilities reported but no confirmed nationwide outages yet.
  • Maritime / Naval: Iranian frigate destroyed by submarine; second Iranian ship in contested area with foreign crew safety concerns.
  • Energy: Attacks on Gulf energy sites and Strait of Hormuz disruptions risk immediate impact on global oil markets.

Regional Political & Military Dynamics

  • Diplomatic posture: EU and GCC leaders have publicly condemned Iranian attacks; Qatar–Iran relations strained by recent arrests.
  • Proxy escalation: Hezbollah indicated increased battlefield engagement risk; US engagement with Kurdish elements noted.
  • International military options: Some partners signalling possible participation; risk of wider coalition action.

Analyst Assessment & Forecast

  • Escalation risk: High in urban Iranian targets and Gulf airspace; further cross-border strikes likely in short term.
  • 72-hour outlook: Expect continued missile/drone launches, targeted strikes on infrastructure, and intensified air-defence activity.
  • Secondary effects: Civilian casualty surge, disruption to civil services, maritime interdictions, and energy market volatility.

Recommended Actions

  • Operational: Increase ISR over Parand/Tehran, Doha flight approaches, and Nakhchivan corridor; validate civilian-impact reports.
  • Diplomatic: Activate deconfliction channels with Gulf partners; monitor indicators of diplomatic rupture (Qatar–Iran).
  • Humanitarian / Civilian Protection: Pre-position warning and medical support messaging; prepare contingency for displaced civilians and infrastructure outages.

Source Note

  • Primary reporting consolidated from regional media feeds and incident videos; tactical details pending further ISR confirmation.

EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

  • US–Israel kinetic campaign has escalated into multi-domain strikes against Iran, producing cross-border effects across the Gulf and Caucasus.
  • Civilian infrastructure damaged; confirmed strikes on educational facilities near Tehran/Parand.
  • Regional escalation vectors active: missile barrages, unmanned aerial systems, and maritime engagements.

THREATS & KINETIC EVENTS

  • Missile strikes: Reports indicate Iranian missile launches targeting Qatar/Doha (audible explosions over Doha) and continued strikes across the Gulf region.
  • US–Israel strikes on Iran: Follow-on attacks include a high-casualty event six days prior (reported 165 KIA) and subsequent strikes reportedly hitting two schools near Tehran/Parand.
  • Drone operations: Suspected Iranian drones struck an airport in Nakhchivan (Azerbaijan exclave); Tehran publicly denies responsibility while regional leaders seek apology.
  • Indirect/Proxy actions: Drones targeted a US base in Iraq; reports of downed drones and explosions in multiple Gulf states indicate contagion across theaters.
  • Hezbollah re-engagement signaled — increased risk of northern front openings against Israel from Lebanon.
  • US engagement with Kurdish groups in Iran/Iraq noted as potential low-intensity irregular threat vector inside Iran.

CIVILIAN & CRITICAL INFRASTRUCTURE IMPACT

  • Education: Two schools near Tehran/Parand struck — civilian casualty and protection concerns.
  • Communications: Internet disruptions reported inside Iran; situational awareness and information flow degraded.
  • Utilities: Local water and electricity facilities sustained damage; no nationwide outages yet but fragility increased.
  • Maritime/Shipping: Strait of Hormuz disruptions and attacks on energy infrastructure driving elevated commercial risk.
  • Humanitarian: Evacuation/medical surge risk in urban centers where strikes occurred.

REGIONAL POLITICAL & DIPLOMATIC DYNAMICS

  • Qatar: Arrests of alleged IRGC cell personnel have strained bilateral ties and may reduce Doha's mediation options.
  • Major powers: Moscow and Beijing publicly condemn attacks but have so far avoided kinetic support — diplomatic distance limits rapid de-escalation.
  • Coalition risk: Statements from allied capitals (Canada ambiguous on participation) increase unpredictability of coalition composition and timelines.

ECONOMIC & GLOBAL EFFECTS

  • Energy markets: Attacks on Gulf energy sites and shipping routes are creating acute oil-price volatility and supply-chain risk for exporters/importers.
  • Global events: Major sports and international events disrupted due to travel, security, and participant safety concerns.

OPERATIONAL INDICATORS & INTELLIGENCE GAPS

  • Indicators observed: increased missile/rocket launches, UAS launches from Iranian territory, uptick in maritime interdiction attempts.
  • Gaps: Attribution uncertainty for some UAS strikes (denials issued by Tehran); limited on-the-ground confirmation for casualty counts in several incidents.
  • Escalation triggers: Targeting of civilian infrastructure, maritime commerce interdiction, and cross-border proxy openings (Hezbollah, Kurdish axes).

RECOMMENDED ACTIONS

  • Raise maritime and airspace alerts for commercial and allied transit in Gulf and adjacent corridors; prioritize convoy protection through Strait of Hormuz.
  • Increase SIGINT/IMINT collection focused on Tehran, known UAS launch corridors, and Nakhchivan routes to clarify attribution.
  • Coordinate with humanitarian partners to preposition medical and shelter support near impacted urban centers (Tehran/Parand vicinity).
  • Monitor diplomatic channels with Qatar, Russia, and China for changes in posture that could affect de-escalation windows.
  • Maintain readiness for rapid force protection and base hardening at forward-located US and coalition sites in Iraq and the Levant.

CONCLUSION

  • Conflict remains in an active, multi-domain phase with high risk of geographic expansion. Immediate protective measures recommended for identified hotspots.

Executive Summary

  • Rapid escalation: combined US–Israel kinetic strikes against Iran and reciprocal Iranian regional strikes create multi-front instability.
  • High-civilian risk: reported strikes have impacted educational facilities and critical infrastructure; communications intermittently disrupted.
  • Regional spillover: incidents reported in Qatar (Doha), Azerbaijan (Nakhchivan), Iraq and Lebanon.

Current Threats / Active Hostilities

  • Tehran / Parand: missile strikes reported; two schools hit according to Iranian media; prior single attack killed 165.
  • Doha, Qatar: reported barrage of Iranian missiles and loud explosions over the city.
  • Nakhchivan, Azerbaijan: suspected Iranian drone strike at airport; Azerbaijan claims right to respond.
  • US bases in Iraq: drone strikes reported against US facilities.
  • Lebanon: Hezbollah leadership pledges renewed engagement against Israel; risk of north–south escalation.
  • Maritime: Iranian warship destroyed by US submarine; follow-on Iran warship activity prompting Sri Lankan safeguarding efforts.

Recent Incidents (chronological / by report)

  • Missiles hit two schools near Tehran — Iranian media report.
  • US & Israeli missiles reported striking Iranian targets.
  • Doha explosions — barrage of Iranian missiles reported over Qatar.
  • Drone attack at Azerbaijan (Nakhchivan) airport; Iran denies responsibility.
  • Drone strike on US base in Iraq reported; regional downing of drones noted.
  • Iranian attacks on regional assets continue; Gulf Arab states report explosions and losses.
  • Internet restrictions reported inside Iran concurrent with bombardment; utilities reportedly damaged.
  • Iranian frigate destroyed by US submarine; dozens of sailors killed.
  • Second Iranian warship in same area — Sri Lanka reports safeguarding efforts onboard.
  • Qatar IRGC cell arrests — arrests reported causing diplomatic strain between Qatar and Iran.
  • Hezbollah leader reaffirms continued fight; commentary on strategic gamble.
  • Energy-target attacks in Gulf; disruptions to Strait of Hormuz and regional energy infrastructure reported.
  • Global impacts: major sports events and travel disrupted; insurance and commercial risks rising.
  • Cuba: massive power outage reported amid oil/energy pressure consequences.
  • Gaza flotilla activists confronted by Tunisian police at Sidi Bou Said port.
  • Political messaging: US congressional vs executive war-declaration debate referenced; Canadian PM non-committal on military involvement.
  • Great power posture: Moscow and Beijing condemn strikes rhetorically but decline military support.
  • Information environment: analysis pieces on Kurdish calculations, rule-of-law debates, and strategic narratives proliferating.
  • Economic: commentary on potential US advantage from oil price shifts and broader global energy market impacts.
  • Miscellaneous: map/context pieces on Iran size and World Cup participation questions tied to conflict.

Strategic Impacts

  • Civilian risk spike: strikes on schools and infrastructure increase potential for domestic unrest and refugee flows.
  • Regional escalation: multiple theaters (Gulf, Levant, Caucasus, Iraq) show cross-border kinetic activity; risk of miscalculation is high.
  • Supply chain and energy: attacks on energy sites and Strait of Hormuz threaten global energy supply and shipping insurance rates.
  • Diplomatic fractures: arrests and naval incidents stress Qatar–Iran relations and complicate mediation channels.
  • Information operations: denial and competing narratives are active; strategic messaging from state and non-state actors intensifies.

Recommended Immediate Actions

  • Raise regional alert to IMMEDIATE for civilian-protection actors in Tehran, Doha, Nakhchivan and Iraqi base vicinities.
  • Deploy additional collection on maritime movements near the area where the Iranian frigate was destroyed; monitor warship transits.
  • Increase SIGINT/OSINT monitoring for communications blackouts and extremist amplification inside Iran and Lebanon.
  • Coordinate with diplomatic channels to assess Qatar–Iran rift and potential safe-passage requirements for foreign nationals.
  • Advise commercial partners to re-route shipping and adjust energy exposure until transit security stabilizes.

Intelligence Gaps / Requirements

  • Confirm attribution for the Nakhchivan airport strike and whether strikes cross into NATO-partner airspaces.
  • Obtain verified casualty figures and infrastructure damage reports for Parand/Tehran strikes.
  • Track location and intent of remaining Iranian naval assets and any coalition naval tasking in the area.
  • Assess Hezbollah force posture changes inside Lebanon and command-and-control linkages to Iranian direction.
  • Monitor diplomatic communications from Moscow and Beijing for rapid policy shifts or covert support indicators.

Bottom Line

  • Multi-domain conflict expansion is ongoing; prioritize protection of civilians, critical infrastructure, and maritime trade routes. Expect continued kinetic activity and heightened risk of miscalculation.

Situation Overview

  • US & Israel conducting coordinated strikes inside Iran; continued multi-day kinetic campaign.
  • Iran conducting retaliatory strikes across the region against US assets, Israel, and regional targets.

Recent Significant Events

  • Drone strike on Nakhchivan (Azerbaijan) airport — eyewitness video circulated; Iran denies responsibility.
  • Ballistic missile intercept by NATO over eastern Mediterranean after reported trajectory toward Turkey; launch attribution disputed.
  • Drone strikes against a US base in Iraq — force-protection breach; pattern of attacks persists.
  • Israeli strikes in Beirut (southern suburbs) — reported fatalities (8) and strikes on Iranian-linked targets.
  • Iran directing operations against Kurdish groups in Iraq and initiating fresh strikes on Israel.
  • Sri Lankan vessel rerouted to area after a US submarine engagement destroyed an Iranian frigate — heightened maritime hazard.
  • Qatar arrests tied to an IRGC cell; diplomatic friction with Tehran increased.
  • Internet disruptions and localized damage to water and electricity infrastructure inside Iran reported; no confirmed nationwide blackout yet.
  • Energy market risk elevated — regional supply chokepoints threatened; price volatility expected.
  • Hezbollah public recommitment to combat operations; Lebanon front risk elevated.
  • Increased reports of settler violence against Palestinian communities under cover of regional hostilities.

Operational Impact

  • Civil infrastructure in affected Iranian provinces at elevated risk; water and power systems require monitoring.
  • Maritime security degraded — commercial and naval transits in Gulf and adjacent sea lanes face elevated threat.
  • Force protection needs escalation for bases in Iraq and forward sites; counter-UAS and missile defenses prioritized.
  • Regional escalation probability high given multi-axis engagements and proxy mobilization.

Forecast & Recommended Actions

  • Plan for sustained kinetic activity over the next 72–96 hours; track Iranian drone and ballistic launch patterns.
  • Advise civil contingency plans for localized utility outages and medical surge capacity in affected areas.
  • Increase maritime escorts and consider rerouting commercial shipping away from high-risk corridors where feasible.
  • Enhance air defenses and deploy counter-UAS at critical infrastructure and bases in Iraq, Qatar, and Gulf states.
  • Maintain diplomatic de-escalation channels with external stakeholders (Russia, China) and monitor public statements for shifts in posture.

Executive Summary

  • Nature: State-directed information operations by the Russian Federation targeting Ukrainian identity, language, culture and occupied territories.
  • Primary Source: EUvsDisinfo reporting (detailed below).

Observed Items

  • 2026-03-02 — Russia’s Information Grip on Ukraine’s Occupied Territories; link; Impact: Consolidation of narrative control in occupied areas to weaken Ukrainian governance and social cohesion.
  • 2026-02-27 — Russia’s quest for disinformation gold; link; Impact: Systematic harvesting and amplification of false narratives to influence domestic and international perception.
  • 2026-02-24 — Total Recall: Russian attempts to erase the Ukrainian language; link; Impact: Targeted linguistic suppression to degrade national identity and institutional continuity.
  • 2026-02-23 — Total Recall: Russian attempts to erase the Ukrainian culture; link; Impact: Cultural attrition campaigns aimed at normalization of occupation.
  • 2026-02-22 — Total Recall: How Russia tried to erase the Ukrainian identity; link; Impact: Long-term identity-targeting operations to justify territorial control and diminish resistance.

Assessment

  • Threat Level: High — coordinated information operations are a force-multiplier for territorial control and political suppression.
  • Actors: Russian state media and proxy networks employing layered disinformation and cultural engineering.
  • Vulnerabilities: Populations in occupied territories; language and cultural institutions susceptible to narrative manipulation.

Recommended Actions

  • Immediate: Enhance verification, targeted counter-messaging, and material support for local Ukrainian language and cultural resilience programs.
  • Operational: Track and attribute dissemination networks; restrict amplification channels; prioritize rapid rebuttal to high-impact false narratives.
  • Intelligence: Task HUMINT and technical collection to identify operational nodes and platform coordination originating from Russian-controlled areas.

Situation Overview

  • Actor: Russian state-directed information operations targeting occupied Ukrainian territories.
  • Scope: Systematic campaigns to erase Ukrainian language, culture, and national identity.
  • Source: EUvsDisinfo reporting (22 Feb – 02 Mar 2026).

Recent Identified Campaigns

  • 02 Mar 2026: "Russia’s Information Grip on Ukraine’s Occupied Territories" — consolidation of information control; source.
  • 27 Feb 2026: "Russia’s quest for disinformation gold" — amplification networks and monetized disinformation; source.
  • 24 Feb 2026: "Total Recall: Russian attempts to erase the Ukrainian language" — targeted language suppression operations; source.
  • 23 Feb 2026: "Total Recall: Russian attempts to erase the Ukrainian culture" — cultural erasure narratives and heritage manipulation; source.
  • 22 Feb 2026: "Total Recall: How Russia tried to erase the Ukrainian identity" — identity-denial messaging and administrative measures; source.

Locations of Active Threat

  • Occupied Crimea: Information control and cultural assimilation operations actively enforced.
  • Donetsk & Luhansk oblasts: Persistent messaging campaigns to delegitimize Ukrainian identity and language.
  • Kherson & Zaporizhzhia: Administrative integration narratives, media takeover, and suppression of Ukrainian-language outlets.

Impact Assessment

  • Civilian population: Reduced access to independent Ukrainian-language media; elevated informational coercion risk.
  • Information environment: High prevalence of state-directed disinformation; increased polarization and identity displacement.
  • Operational risk: Information dominance used to justify administrative control and inhibit organized resistance.

Recommended Immediate Actions

  • Priority: Restore and secure independent Ukrainian-language distribution channels into occupied territories.
  • Countermeasures: Execute targeted counter-messaging, disrupt amplification networks, and digitally preserve cultural records.
  • Intelligence: Increase OSINT collection cadence, verify narratives with local civil society, and map actor infrastructure for follow-on actions.

Threat Assessment

  • Intent: High — strategic objective to assimilate occupied populations and delegitimize Ukrainian statehood through sustained information operations.
  • Capability: High — coordinated state-level resources, platform manipulation, and institutional takeover provide persistent reach into occupied territories.
  • Immediacy: Ongoing — activities observed across multiple domains (media, education, administrative communications) indicating active implementation rather than isolated messaging.

Operational Impact

  • Societal: Erosion of Ukrainian language use, cultural practices, and civic identity in occupied areas; increased risk of forced assimilation.
  • Stability: Reduced trust in local institutions; higher likelihood of localized compliance or collaboration due to controlled information environment.
  • Information Environment: Degraded information integrity — false narratives normalized, independent media suppressed, civic counter-narratives marginalized.

  • Priority: Support rapid information resilience programs in at-risk communities — emergency distribution of verified Ukrainian-language content and secure communication channels.

  • Civic Support: Protect cultural and educational institutions through documentation, remote instruction support, and preservation of language curricula archives.
  • Signals Intelligence: Monitor administrative changes (documents, school curricula, local legislation) as indicators of deeper integration efforts.

Indicators for Continued Monitoring

  • Administrative: New local decrees replacing Ukrainian language or cultural references; personnel changes installing pro-Kremlin administrators.

  • Education: Curriculum edits removing Ukrainian historical, linguistic, or cultural content.

Sources

Analyst Note

  • Operational posture: Treat information-led cultural erasure as a long-term occupation enabler. Prioritize measures that preserve language and identity integrity while degrading adversary amplification capability.

Packagist Supply‑Chain RAT — Tactical Summary

  • Malicious packages identified on Packagist: nhattuanbl/lara-helper, nhattuanbl/simple-queue, nhattuanbl/lara-swagger (distribution via Composer dependency chaining).
  • Behavior: cross‑platform RAT (Windows, macOS, Linux) activated at application boot or autoload; runs in web app process with application privileges.
  • Capabilities: system reconnaissance, remote shell execution (fallback chain: popen, proc_open, exec, shell_exec, system, passthru), file read/write, exfiltration to C2.
  • Command & Control: connects to helper.leuleu[.]net:2096 over TCP, persistent retry loop (~15s).
  • Operational note: threat actor published benign libraries (e.g., nhattuanbl/lara-media, snooze, syslog) to build credibility.
  • Immediate actions: assume compromise if packages present; remove packages, rotate all environment secrets, audit outbound traffic to helper.leuleu[.]net.

Silver Dragon / APT41 — Operational Summary

  • Targeting: primarily government entities in Europe and Southeast Asia; specific campaign targeting Uzbekistan noted.
  • TTPs: initial access via exposed servers and phishing; persistence via legitimate Windows service hijack; payloads include Cobalt Strike beacons and DNS tunneling C2.
  • Infection chains documented: AppDomain hijacking, Service DLL, and LNK/PowerShell phishing deployments.
  • Loaders/tools: MonikerLoader (in‑memory .NET loader), BamboLoader (service DLL, shellcode decrypter/injector), and sideload via GameHook.exe to launch Cobalt Strike.
  • Unique C2 technique: backdoor authenticates to attacker Google Drive account, using file uploads/downloads (different extensions signal tasks/results).
  • Immediate actions: harden public‑facing servers, block known post‑exploitation tooling patterns, monitor unusual Google Drive uploads/downloads, and hunt for service DLL hijacks and persistence artifacts.

Attribution

Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures (TTPs)

  • Initial vector: inbox flooding (spam) to prompt engagement, followed by voice contact from actors posing as IT desk.
  • Social engineering to obtain remote access via Quick Assist or install of tools such as AnyDesk.
  • Credential harvesting via fake Microsoft Outlook anti-spam landing page hosted on AWS with password overlay.
  • Malware delivery: download of legitimate binaries (e.g., ADNotificationManager.exe, DLPUserAgent.exe, Werfault.exe) used to DLL-sideload malicious payloads.
  • Payload: vcruntime140_1.dll implements obfuscation, timing delays, and uses Hell's Gate/Halo's Gate techniques to hook ntdll.dll and evade EDR.
  • Post-compromise: deployment of Havoc Demon and use of legitimate RMM tools (Level RMM, XEOX) for persistence and redundancy.

Indicators of Compromise (IOCs) and Artifacts

  • Malware/Agents: Havoc Demon, Havoc C2.

  • Tools/Channels: AnyDesk, Quick Assist, Level RMM, XEOX; voice contact claiming to be IT support.
  • Infrastructure: phishing landing pages hosted on AWS impersonating Microsoft services.
  • Persistence Mechanisms: scheduled tasks to relaunch Havoc Demon; legitimate RMM installations used as fallback persistence.

Operational Impact

  • Rapid lateral movement observed: example case of compromise moving to nine additional endpoints within ~11 hours.

  • Enforce out-of-band verification for remote-support requests; block unsolicited remote-access sessions.

  • Rotate credentials and enforce multi-factor authentication on all accounts; prioritize forensic review of accounts accessed during the window of compromise.
  • Pursue takedown and reporting of AWS-hosted phishing infrastructure; coordinate with cloud provider for rapid removal.
